name: Gantt Critical/High Tasks Due Soon Emailer (V2-UI captured via Pipeline A)
description: Captured from the V2 UI agent-creation flow on 2026-05-17 AFTER the pivot from Pipeline B to Pipeline A (commit pending — feature/v6-v2-integration branch). Same user prompt as `gantt-urgent-tasks-v2ui` (which captured Pipeline B output), so the two scenarios serve as a direct A-vs-B comparison: same EP → different IRs → different DSLs. Pipeline A produces an 11-step deterministic workflow (vs Pipeline B's 6 with AI-heavy filtering) and runs end-to-end with correct HTML output. Locked here as the first V2-UI + Pipeline A regression scenario.

expected.phase_e_notes: End-to-end pass: 12/12 steps, real email delivered to meiribarak@gmail.com with 3 Critical/High priority tasks due in the next 3 days. Requires all five runtime fixes from 2026-05-17 to be in place: WP-39/40/41/42/43 (committed in 33474bb) AND WP-45/46 (in the same commit as this scenario). Run via Mode B: --use-db-dsl. Note: agent_configurations.input_values must be populated for `{{input.X}}` config refs to resolve at runtime.

comparison_with_pipeline_b.key_takeaway: Pipeline A produces a more deterministic, cheaper, format-correct workflow with the same input. WP-43, WP-44 are entirely sidestepped by Pipeline A's IC prompt fidelity. Surfaced WP-45 + WP-46 in shared runtime — fixed defensively (also benefit Pipeline B).

known_weaknesses.symptom: ConditionalEvaluator's `gte`/`lte` did not (a) defensively resolve bare-string variable references (e.g. `"date_window.window_start"` was treated as a literal), or (b) perform date-aware comparison (lexicographic string compare across DD/MM/YYYY vs ISO date string). Two independent gaps in `lib/pilot/ConditionalEvaluator.ts` — WP-29/30/31 hardened the W2 expression evaluator's parser but did not touch ConditionalEvaluator's own private parser.
known_weaknesses.note: Three-part fix: looksLikeBareVariableRef() helper generalizes WP-22's defensive wrap to all comparison operators; compareAsDates() makes gte/lte/gt/lt date-aware via the canonical WP-29 parseDate; private parseDate delegates to the shared parser. Surfaces a new anti-pattern: 'runtime helpers that look the same should BE the same' — duplicate parseDate implementations diverged.

known_weaknesses.symptom: Pipeline A's IC LLM emitted `transform/with_fields` with all field expressions being constants (no `item.*` refs) — intent: 'compute these named constants'. But `with_fields` is documented as per-item augmentation: input array of 57 → output array of 57 each augmented with the same constants. Downstream filter expected `date_window` to be a singleton object — got an array, `.window_start` access returned undefined, filter dropped all rows silently. Same anti-pattern shape as WP-41 (LLM picked transform whose semantics don't match the intended use).
known_weaknesses.note: Runtime tolerance: when ALL field expressions are constant (walked via isConstantExpression() helper), emit singleton object regardless of input array length. Per-item behavior unchanged when any field references item.*.

known_weaknesses.symptom: INSERT into agent_configurations was missing explicit `id` field; the NOT NULL constraint silently failed, causing every --use-db-dsl run to wipe the table without repopulating it.
known_weaknesses.note: Added crypto.randomUUID() for the id field on insert. Pre-existing latent bug — hidden until Pipeline A's config-refs design exposed the dependency.
